planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 2–3 inches deep in spring after the last frost; established clumps expand steadily and are divided every 3–4 years. In zone 7–9 myoga grows as a fully perennial ground cover in part shade to full shade, dying back to the ground each winter and re-emerging reliably in spring. The harvest season for the edible flower buds is August–September in zone 9.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions 2–3 inches deep in rich, moist, humus-rich soil in part shade to full shade; Japanese ginger (myoga) is a shade-thriving edible perennial rarely seen in American gardens despite being widely grown in Japan for its prized flower buds. The plant's ginger-scented strap leaves reach 24–36 inches in shade conditions, forming a handsome ground cover through the growing season. The harvest is the unopened flower buds, which emerge at soil level in August–September — these small, pink-tipped buds are sliced thin and used as a pungent, mildly spicy garnish in miso soup, sashimi, noodle dishes, and pickled in vinegar. Young spring shoots are also edible with a more delicate flavor. The flavor is distinctly gingery but milder and more aromatic than culinary ginger root. Plants naturalize slowly and are nearly care-free once established in a shaded spot with consistent moisture.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Edible Ginger Relatives
Japanese Culinary Herb Society — Myoga Cultivation

common_name: King of the Garden Lima Bean

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 65°F; in zone 9 plant March–May for spring and July–August for fall. King of the Garden is a vigorous pole lima requiring a sturdy 6–8 foot trellis; it continues producing through zone 9 summer heat better than most pole beans and yields heavily in the warm fall.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep at the base of a sturdy trellis or fence after soil warms; King of the Garden is a 19th-century heirloom pole lima bean that produces the largest seeds of any standard lima variety — big, flat, creamy white beans with outstanding buttery flavor preferred for both fresh shelling and drying. The vigorous, long-season vines climb to 6–8 feet and produce pods continuously from midsummer through frost, making the per-plant yield among the highest of any climbing bean. Fresh-shelled beans are harvested when the pods are plump and bright green; dried beans require extended cooking time but have exceptional keeping quality. Inoculate seeds with lima bean-specific rhizobia at planting for nitrogen fixation in new garden soil.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — King of the Garden Lima Bean
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Pole Lima Beans
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Lima Bean Types

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants or rooted cuttings in spring or fall; Cherokee Rose establishes readily and grows vigorously in zone 7–9. The canes are extremely thorny and reach 15–20 feet — site at the back of borders, on fences, or as a naturalized hedge where the thorns provide security. Once established it requires virtually no maintenance.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained soil; Cherokee rose is a large, vigorous climbing rose native to China that naturalized across the Deep South and is now Georgia's state flower. The single, 3–4 inch white flowers with prominent golden stamens are produced in extraordinary abundance in March–April, covering the long canes with a spectacular display that has made this rose a Southern garden tradition for over two centuries. The hips that follow are large, pear-shaped, red, and persist through winter — they are extremely high in vitamin C and were historically made into rosehip syrup and tea. The evergreen to semi-evergreen foliage is attractive year-round and the thorny canes form impenetrable barriers that deter deer and intruders. Requires almost no care once established; thrives on neglect.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Rosa laevigata
University of Georgia Extension — State Wildflower: Cherokee Rose
North Carolina State Extension — Rosa laevigata

planting.note: Direct sow or start indoors 3 weeks before last frost; in zone 9 direct sow February–April for summer harvest or August for fall. Moranga does best with a long, warm growing season and heavy, fertile soil; in zone 9 it benefits from fall planting to avoid peak summer heat during fruit development.

directions: Sow 2–3 seeds per hill 1 inch deep in deeply amended, fertile soil and thin to the strongest plant when 3 inches tall; Moranga is a large, flat-ribbed, orange-red Brazilian pumpkin (Cucurbita maxima) with deep orange, dense, sweet flesh used throughout Brazil as the traditional vessel for stuffed shrimp dishes (camarão na moranga) and as a soup tureen. The large fruits, averaging 15–25 pounds, are oval to flat with a warty, orange skin and extraordinarily thick, starchy-sweet flesh with minimal seed cavity. The Moranga type is closely related to Hubbard squash and has been cultivated in Brazil for centuries. The flesh makes exceptional purées, soups, and savory stuffings. Store cured fruits for 3–4 months in cool conditions.
sources: Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Cucurbita maxima Types
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Large Heirloom Squash

planting.note: Plant divisions or transplants in spring or fall in consistently moist, shaded locations; bigleaf ligularia is not drought-tolerant and wilts dramatically in afternoon heat — in zone 8–9 plant only where reliable moisture and deep shade are available. It performs best in zone 4–7 but can succeed in zone 8–9 in properly sited spots near water features or on north-facing slopes.

directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich soil in deep shade; bigleaf ligularia is a bold shade perennial with large, kidney-shaped, dark green to burgundy leaves reaching 12–16 inches across, and upright orange-yellow daisy flowers on dark stems in July–August. It creates a dramatic tropical effect in shaded wet areas and is stunning at pond margins and woodland stream edges. In zone 8–9 it requires reliable moisture and afternoon shade as it wilts dramatically in heat and sun, recovering overnight; choose cultivars like 'Desdemona' or 'Othello' (bronze-purple foliage) which handle zone 8 heat better than the species. Divide established clumps every 3–4 years to maintain vigor. Slugs are the primary pest.
sources: Missouri Botanical Garden — Ligularia dentata Plant Finder
University of Georgia Extension — Bold Shade Perennials for Southern Gardens
